title: SEGMENT 8 — The Western abandonment: antibiotics, and a hundred-year detour

lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — The Western abandonment: antibiotics, and a hundred-year detour
Why did phage therapy die in the West?
The straightforward answer is that antibiotics happened.

But the more interesting answer involves a combination of antibiotics happening, methodological inadequacy of the early phage work, and a series of high-profile scientific embarrassments that turned the Western medical establishment against the whole field.
The methodological problem first.
D'Hérelle and his early followers were enthusiasts, not clean experimentalists.
They reported successes without adequate controls.
They didn't always know what was in their phage preparations — sometimes the "phage" preparation contained dead bacteria, host debris, endotoxin, who knew what else.
The host range of their phages was often imperfectly characterized, so a preparation might work against one strain and fail against a related strain for reasons nobody understood.
Storage and shelf life were inconsistent — phages are biological entities, and a preparation that worked when fresh might be inert after sitting on a shelf for a year.
Clinical trials, where they were done at all, were often single-arm and uncontrolled.
Reading the early phage therapy literature, you find a lot of "we treated 200 patients and 180 got better" without any control group, without rigorous case definition, without standard dosing.
It's not that the results were wrong — many were probably real.
It's that they were not convincing by the methodological standards developing in mid-twentieth-century American medicine.
In 1934, the Council on Pharmacy and Chemistry of the American Medical Association commissioned a critical review of phage therapy by two researchers, Krueger and Scribner.

They evaluated the evidence and concluded, more or less, that phage therapy might have something to it in certain narrow applications but that the bulk of the published work was scientifically inadequate, that the results were inconsistent, and that the mechanism by which phages were supposed to work in tissues remained obscure.
The review was not a categorical dismissal, but it was a strong statement of doubt, and it landed on American physicians' desks just as a much shinier alternative was emerging.
That alternative, of course, was sulfonamides in the late 1930s and then penicillin in the 1940s.
Antibiotics had everything phages didn't.
They were small molecules, chemically defined, stable on the shelf, predictable in dose-response, broad-spectrum, and produced industrially with consistent quality.

You didn't have to match a particular antibiotic to a particular patient's bug — penicillin worked on essentially anything gram-positive, sulfonamides worked on a broad swath of bacteria, and you could prescribe empirically without waiting for culture results.
The contrast with phage therapy was brutal.
Phage required matching, required culture, required stocks of dozens of specific phages, and worked only against the exact bug it was matched to.
Antibiotics required none of that.
By 1950, in the United States and Western Europe, phage therapy was essentially dead.
The companies that had been producing phage products in the 1920s and 30s — Eli Lilly had a small phage product line, as did several European pharmaceutical houses — discontinued them.
Medical schools stopped teaching phage therapy.

The Soviet tradition kept going behind the Iron Curtain, but Western doctors didn't read Russian and weren't inclined to take seriously a therapeutic tradition associated with what they regarded as a backward state medical system.
Phages went into hibernation in the West.
For sixty years, they survived in molecular biology labs as research tools — that's the line we picked up in Segment 5 — but as therapeutics they were essentially extinct in the Western pharmacopeia.
And then antibiotic resistance happened, and the calculation flipped.
